conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances.

Let me start with: **Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you inquiring about?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have two children. I'm inquiring about both of them - Alex, who is 12, and Blake, who is 6.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! So you have two children - Alex (12 years old) and Blake (6 years old).

To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for both Alex and Blake, I need to ask you a few questions.

**First, are you responsible for both Alex and Blake? By this I mean, do they live with you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, it's a bit complicated. Alex doesn't live with me - he lives with his other parent. Blake does live with me, but I'm actually fostering Blake, and the local authority gives me maintenance for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for explaining that. This is helpful information.

Let me ask about Alex first: **Is the other parent that Alex lives with currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I'm not entirely sure if they're claiming it or not. I do help out a bit with Alex's expenses - I contribute towards things like clothes and bits and pieces, but it's not a huge amount.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. That's helpful to know about your contributions to Alex's expenses.

Now, regarding Blake - you mentioned you're fostering Blake and the local authority gives you maintenance. **Is this a formal foster care arrangement through the local authority, or is it an informal/kinship care arrangement?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, it's a formal foster care arrangement through the local authority. They provide me with payments for Blake's care.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that.

Now, let me ask about your circumstances: **Are you currently living in the UK, and are you responsible for either Alex or Blake in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm here in England. And yes, I'm responsible for both children in the sense that I contribute to Alex's upkeep even though he doesn't live with me, and I care for Blake who lives with me here.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for confirming that. Just a couple more questions to complete the assessment.

**Regarding Alex - even though you contribute to his expenses, would you say that Alex's other parent is the one who has the main day-to-day responsibility for Alex since Alex lives with them full-time?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right. Since Alex lives with them full-time, they have the main day-to-day responsibility for him. I just help out financially where I can.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Based on what you've told me, I've assessed your eligibility for Child Benefit for both Alex and Blake.

**Unfortunately, you are not eligible to claim Child Benefit for either child. Here's why:**

**For Alex (age 12):**
Since Alex lives with his other parent full-time and they have the main day-to-day responsibility for him, you would not be eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex. Only one person can claim Child Benefit per child, and this is usually the person the child lives with. The other parent would be the eligible person to claim Child Benefit for Alex (if they're not already doing so). While it's great that you contribute financially to Alex's expenses, this doesn't make you eligible for Child Benefit when the child lives elsewhere.

**For Blake (age 6):**
Although Blake lives with you, you cannot claim Child Benefit for a child who is being looked after by the local authority in formal care. Since Blake is in a formal foster care arrangement through the local authority and you receive maintenance payments from them for Blake's care, you are not eligible for Child Benefit. This rule applies to children who are in local authority care.

I understand this may be disappointing, but I hope this explanation helps clarify your situation. If you have any questions about this decision, please let me know.

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 12 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 6 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 12 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 12 weeks (within 12-week limit)
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 6 (under 16)
  - Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is fostered and the council pays for their accommodation or maintenance
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
circumstances: Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
Child is not in local authority care
Child in hospital for 12 weeks (within 12-week limit)
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Another claimant who lives with the child has priority; Child is fostered and the council pays for their accommodation or maintenance
circumstances: Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child is fostered and the council pays for their accommodation or maintenance
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 12 years old (under 16) and lives with his other parent who has main day-to-day responsibility for him. According to the Child Benefit rules, only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child, and this is usually the person the child lives with. Since Alex doesn't live with the claimant and the other parent has main responsibility, the claimant would not be eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex. The other parent would be the eligible person to claim (if they're not already doing so).

Blake is 6 years old (under 16) and lives with the claimant. However, Blake is in a formal foster care arrangement through the local authority, and the claimant receives maintenance payments from the local authority for Blake's care. According to the Child Benefit rules, you cannot get Child Benefit for a child who is being looked after by the local authority (in care). Foster carers receiving local authority payments are not eligible for Child Benefit for those foster children.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant has two children - Alex (12) and Blake (6). For Alex, the claimant is not eligible because Alex lives with his other parent who has main day-to-day responsibility, and only one person can claim Child Benefit per child. For Blake, despite living with the claimant, the claimant is not eligible because Blake is formally fostered through the local authority with maintenance payments provided, and Child Benefit cannot be claimed for children in local authority care.
